title: Delirium, Dementia, and Depression in Older Adults: The Clinical 3Ds

sections.title: Learning Objectives
content: 1. Differentiate delirium, dementia, and depression using DSM-5 diagnostic criteria, onset patterns, course, and clinical features.
2. Apply the Confusion Assessment Method (CAM) algorithm to accurately identify delirium at the bedside.
3. Administer and interpret the Geriatric Depression Scale-15 (GDS-15) with attention to somatic masking in older adults.
4. Describe hypoactive delirium and explain why it is the most commonly missed subtype in clinical practice.
5. Distinguish pseudodementia (depression presenting as cognitive impairment) from true dementia using onset, course, and response-to-treatment criteria.
6. Explain the clinical and management implications when all three conditions coexist simultaneously in a single patient.
7. Implement evidence-based, non-pharmacological nursing interventions to prevent delirium and manage overlapping presentations.

subsections.title: Delirium — Full Clinical Picture
subsections.content:
Delirium is an acute neuropsychiatric syndrome characterized by disturbances in attention, awareness, and cognition that develop over a short period and tend to fluctuate throughout the day. It represents global cerebral dysfunction secondary to one or more identifiable medical causes. The DSM-5 defines delirium using five core criteria: (A) disturbance in attention (reduced ability to direct, focus, sustain, and shift attention) and awareness; (B) acute onset with fluctuating course; (C) additional disturbance in cognition (memory, orientation, language, visuospatial ability, or perception); (D) the disturbances are not better explained by a pre-existing neurocognitive disorder; and (E) the disturbance is a direct physiological consequence of another medical condition, substance intoxication or withdrawal, or exposure to a toxin.

Prevalence: 14–56% of hospitalized older adults; up to 80% in ICU patients. Delirium is an independent predictor of prolonged hospitalization, functional decline, institutionalization, cognitive decline, and mortality.

Subtypes:
- Hyperactive (25%): agitated, combative, pulling at lines, attempting to climb out of bed, loud, confused. Easier to recognize but LESS common.
- Hypoactive (50%): quiet, withdrawn, somnolent, slow to respond, decreased movement. MOST COMMON, MOST MISSED. Mistaken for depression, sedation, or dementia baseline.
- Mixed (25%): fluctuates between hyperactive and hypoactive features.

The THINK Mnemonic for Delirium Causes:
- T — Toxic substances (medications — the #1 cause in older adults)
- H — Hypoxia (pneumonia, PE, HF, COPD exacerbation)
- I — Infection/Immobility (UTI, sepsis, pressure injury)
- N — Non-pharmacological interventions needed (dehydration, malnutrition, sensory impairment)
- K — K+ and other electrolyte/metabolic abnormalities (hyponatremia is most common)

Additional causes: pain, urinary retention, fecal impaction, post-operative state, CVA, head trauma, hepatic encephalopathy, hypoglycemia, hyperthyroidism/hypothyroidism, alcohol withdrawal.

subsections.title: CAM Algorithm — Confusion Assessment Method
subsections.content:
The CAM (Inouye et al., 1990) is the gold-standard bedside delirium screening tool. Sensitivity 94–100%, Specificity 90–95% when used correctly. DELIRIUM IS PRESENT when Features 1 AND 2 are present, PLUS either Feature 3 OR Feature 4.

Feature 1 — Acute Onset AND Fluctuating Course: Is there evidence of an acute change in mental status from the patient's baseline? Does the behavior fluctuate during the day (e.g., come and go, or change in severity)? Answer must be YES to both.

Feature 2 — Inattention: Does the patient have difficulty focusing attention? Distracted easily? Can they complete a serial subtraction or squeeze your hand only when they hear the letter 'A' in a random letter sequence? REQUIRED — without inattention, it is NOT delirium.

Feature 3 — Disorganized Thinking: Is thinking disorganized or incoherent? Rambling, irrelevant conversation, unclear flow of ideas, unpredictable topic switching? Can answer simple logic questions incorrectly (e.g., 'Can a stone float on water?').

Feature 4 — Altered Level of Consciousness: Is the level of consciousness anything other than ALERT? Scored: Vigilant (hyperalert), Lethargic (drowsy but arousable), Stuporous (difficult to arouse), Comatose.

CAM-ICU is the validated version for non-verbal ICU patients using the RASS (Richmond Agitation-Sedation Scale) and standardized features 1–4 adapted for non-verbal assessment.

subsections.title: Dementia — Full Clinical Picture
subsections.content:
Major Neurocognitive Disorder (NCD) — DSM-5 criteria: (A) Evidence of significant cognitive decline from a previous level of performance in one or more cognitive domains (learning and memory, language, executive function, complex attention, perceptual-motor, social cognition) based on: concern of the individual, knowledgeable informant, or clinician; AND objective evidence of substantial cognitive impairment on neuropsychological testing (typically >2 SD below norm); (B) The cognitive deficits interfere with independence in everyday activities; (C) The cognitive deficits do not occur exclusively in the context of delirium; (D) Not better explained by another mental disorder.

Minor NCD (MCI) uses the same domains but: modest (not significant) cognitive decline; deficits do NOT interfere with independence (although compensatory strategies may be used).

Prevalence: 5% of adults 65–70; doubles every 5 years; 1 in 3 adults over 85. Most common cause: Alzheimer's disease (60–80%), followed by vascular dementia, Lewy body dementia, frontotemporal dementia.

Reversible causes (DEMENTIA mnemonic):
- D — Drugs (anticholinergics, benzodiazepines, opioids, antihistamines)
- E — Emotional (depression/pseudodementia)
- M — Metabolic (thyroid, B12, folate, glucose, electrolytes)
- E — Eyes and Ears (sensory deprivation mimicking cognitive impairment)
- N — Nutritional (B12 deficiency, thiamine — Wernicke's)
- T — Trauma/Tumor (subdural hematoma, normal pressure hydrocephalus)
- I — Infection (syphilis, HIV, Lyme disease, CJD)
- A — Arterial/Alcohol (vascular dementia, alcoholic dementia)

subsections.title: Depression in Older Adults — Full Clinical Picture
subsections.content:
Late-life depression (LLD) affects 1–5% of community-dwelling older adults, 10–12% of hospitalized older adults, and up to 40% of nursing home residents. It is UNDERDIAGNOSED because: older adults minimize symptoms ('I'm just tired'), somatic complaints predominate, physicians attribute symptoms to 'normal aging', and older adults rarely volunteer emotional distress.

DSM-5 Major Depressive Episode (MDE) criteria — 5 or more of the following over 2 weeks, representing a change from previous functioning (must include #1 or #2):
1. Depressed mood most of the day, nearly every day
2. Markedly diminished interest or pleasure in all or almost all activities (anhedonia)
3. Significant weight change (loss or gain >5% in 1 month) or change in appetite
4. Insomnia or hypersomnia
5. Psychomotor agitation or retardation observable by others
6. Fatigue or loss of energy
7. Feelings of worthlessness or excessive guilt
8. Diminished ability to concentrate or think
9. Recurrent thoughts of death, suicidal ideation

Masked depression in older adults — somatic presentation: vague pains, GI complaints, fatigue, headache, dizziness. The patient denies sadness but endorses loss of interest, withdrawal, and sleep disturbance. Anhedonia is often the key presenting feature.

GDS-15 (Geriatric Depression Scale, short form):
Administered verbally (yes/no answers). Score ≥5 = positive screen (depression likely). Score ≥10 = significant depression (high specificity).
Key questions: (1) Are you satisfied with your life? (No = 1 point); (2) Have you dropped many activities and interests? (Yes = 1 point); (3) Do you feel your life is empty? (Yes = 1 point); (4) Do you often get bored? (Yes = 1 point); (5) Are you in good spirits most of the time? (No = 1 point); (6) Are you afraid that something bad is going to happen to you? (Yes = 1 point); (7) Do you feel happy most of the time? (No = 1 point); (8) Do you often feel helpless? (Yes = 1 point); (9) Do you prefer to stay at home rather than going out and doing new things? (Yes = 1 point); (10) Do you feel you have more problems with memory than most people? (Yes = 1 point); (11) Do you think it is wonderful to be alive now? (No = 1 point); (12) Do you feel pretty worthless the way you are now? (Yes = 1 point); (13) Do you feel full of energy? (No = 1 point); (14) Do you feel your situation is hopeless? (Yes = 1 point); (15) Do you think that most people are better off than you are? (Yes = 1 point).

subsections.title: Pseudodementia — Depression Masquerading as Dementia
subsections.content:
Pseudodementia (depressive pseudodementia) is a term for the cognitive impairment seen in severe depression that closely mimics dementia. This is a CRITICAL clinical differentiation because the conditions have opposite prognoses: depression is treatable and cognitive impairment resolves.

Key differentiating features:
1. Onset: Pseudodementia — subacute, often following identifiable loss or stressor; Dementia — insidious, gradual
2. Patient awareness: Pseudodementia — patient often COMPLAINS bitterly about memory problems ('I can't remember anything anymore'), is distressed; Dementia — patient often MINIMIZES or is UNAWARE of deficits (anosognosia)
3. Effort on testing: Pseudodementia — frequent 'I don't know' answers, gives up quickly, inconsistent performance; Dementia — genuine attempts but wrong answers, confabulation
4. Cognitive domain pattern: Pseudodementia — global, patchy impairment; Dementia — specific pattern (e.g., short-term memory first in AD)
5. History: Pseudodementia — prior depressive episodes common; Dementia — usually no prior depression
6. Neurological exam: Pseudodementia — normal; Dementia — may show focal signs (especially vascular)
7. Response to treatment: Pseudodementia — cognition IMPROVES with antidepressant treatment; Dementia — does not improve
8. Sleep: Pseudodementia — early morning awakening (3–4 AM), can't get back to sleep; Dementia — sundowning, nighttime wandering
9. Affect: Pseudodementia — consistently dysphoric, tearful, hopeless; Dementia — variable, may be emotionally blunted

Clinical pearl: Even after successful depression treatment, 50% of pseudodementia patients will develop TRUE dementia within 5 years. Depression in later life may be a prodrome or risk factor for dementia. Close follow-up is mandatory.

subsections.title: Hypoactive Delirium vs Depression vs Dementia Baseline
subsections.content:
Hypoactive delirium is the most challenging clinical differentiation in geriatric nursing. The patient appears withdrawn, quiet, sleepy, and disinterested — findings that overlap substantially with depression AND a patient's known dementia baseline.

Key differentiators for hypoactive delirium:
- ACUTE change from baseline (hours to days)
- FLUCTUATION within the day (may have periods of greater clarity)
- INATTENTION: cannot maintain focus, easily distracted, cannot follow multi-step commands
- Physical cause found on investigation (fever, ↑WBC, electrolyte abnormality, new medication, hypoxia)
- CAM is POSITIVE

Mistaking hypoactive delirium for depression: The quiet, withdrawn, non-communicative patient is started on antidepressants when the real problem is sepsis, hyponatremia, or a pulmonary embolism. This delay in treatment is DANGEROUS and potentially fatal.

Mistaking hypoactive delirium for dementia baseline: Staff say 'he/she is always like this' when in fact there has been an acute decline. Caregiver and family interviews are ESSENTIAL — 'Is this different from usual for this patient?'

Nursing imperative: Any ACUTE decline in an older adult's cognition or behavior is delirium until proven otherwise.

subsections.title: Triple Overlap — When Delirium, Dementia, and Depression Coexist
subsections.content:
Dementia is the single greatest risk factor for delirium (5× increased risk). Depression coexists with dementia in 20–40% of cases. In the hospitalized older adult with known dementia and baseline depression, acute delirium superimposed on both creates extreme clinical complexity.

Management priorities when all three coexist:
1. TREAT DELIRIUM FIRST — it is the acute, potentially life-threatening condition
2. Identify and treat the precipitating cause of delirium (infection, medications, metabolic abnormality)
3. Do NOT initiate new antidepressants during active delirium (confounds assessment; many antidepressants worsen delirium, e.g., TCAs due to anticholinergic burden)
4. Maintain cognitive continuity: familiar faces, orientation cues, consistent nursing staff
5. Once delirium resolves, reassess depression and cognitive baseline — these may both be worse post-delirium
6. Post-delirium cognitive assessment: never assess dementia stage during or immediately after delirium (results invalid)
7. Family education: explain that delirium may accelerate the underlying dementia trajectory; 40% do not fully return to baseline
8. Post-delirium depression is common and should be screened for at 4–8 weeks after resolution

Documentation: When all three are present, document each diagnosis separately with supporting clinical evidence. Avoid collapsing all three into 'altered mental status' — this prevents identification of delirium as an acute emergency.

sections.title: Normal Cognitive and Neurological Aging Changes
sections.content:
Understanding normal aging is essential to identifying abnormal findings. Normal aging does NOT cause delirium, dementia, or clinical depression — these are pathological states.

Normal cognitive changes with aging:
- Processing speed: slows significantly (but accuracy is preserved)
- Divided attention: harder to multitask
- Working memory: mild decline (holding and manipulating information)
- Episodic memory: some decline in retrieval speed (tip-of-the-tongue phenomenon is normal)
- Semantic memory: PRESERVED (vocabulary, general knowledge, crystallized intelligence)
- Procedural memory: PRESERVED (how to do learned skills)
- Executive function: mild decline in complex planning

Normal neurological aging:
- Brain volume decreases 0.5% per year after age 60 (primarily frontal lobe)
- White matter changes on MRI (leukoaraiosis) — present in most older adults, correlates with processing speed
- Sleep architecture changes: less deep (stage 3) sleep, more frequent awakenings, earlier sleep onset and awakening
- Slowing of central processing (reflexes slower)
- Mild postural instability

What is NOT normal aging and requires investigation:
- Forgetting important events, appointments, the names of close family members
- Getting lost in familiar places
- Word-finding problems that interfere with communication
- Inability to manage finances, medications, or daily activities
- Personality changes
- Any acute change in mental status

sections.title: Abnormal Findings and Red Flags
sections.content:
Immediate escalation triggers for Delirium:
- Acute mental status change in any hospitalized older adult
- CAM positive
- New restlessness, agitation, or conversely new somnolence/withdrawal
- Hallucinations (especially visual)
- Fever + confusion = sepsis until proven otherwise
- Hypoxia (SpO2 < 90%) with confusion
- New focal neurological signs with confusion (CVA)

Red flags for Dementia (requiring urgent workup):
- Rapid cognitive decline over weeks (CJD, paraneoplastic, normal pressure hydrocephalus)
- Cognitive decline with early falls and gait disturbance (LBD, NPH, vascular)
- Early personality change before memory loss (frontotemporal dementia)
- Cognitive decline in young person (<65): familial AD, HIV, metabolic
- Reversible causes not yet excluded

Red flags for Depression requiring urgent intervention:
- Any expression of suicidal ideation
- Refusal to eat or drink (risk of medical deterioration)
- Psychotic features (delusional guilt, nihilistic delusions)
- GDS-15 ≥ 10
- PHQ-9 ≥ 15
- Severe functional impairment
- New onset depression in >65 without prior history: rule out organic cause (hypothyroidism, B12 deficiency, malignancy)

sections.title: Assessment Tools and Clinical Approach
sections.content:
A systematic approach to the cognitively impaired older adult:

Step 1: Determine if this is ACUTE or CHRONIC
- Acute = delirium until proven otherwise
- Ask family/caregiver: 'Is this new or different from how they usually are?'
- Review nurse's notes for baseline

Step 2: Screen for Delirium — CAM
- Feature 1: Acute onset + fluctuating course (YES required)
- Feature 2: Inattention (YES required)
- Feature 3: Disorganized thinking OR Feature 4: Altered consciousness
- If CAM positive → treat delirium, investigate causes

Step 3: If NOT delirium, screen for Depression — GDS-15
- Score ≥5: positive screen; refer for evaluation
- Score ≥10: significant depression; urgent psychiatric/medical review
- NOTE: GDS not validated during active delirium — wait until resolved

Step 4: Cognitive assessment — MoCA or MMSE
- MoCA ≥ 26: normal; 18–25: MCI range; < 18: dementia range
- MMSE ≥ 24: normal; 18–23: mild dementia; 10–17: moderate; < 10: severe
- Add 1 point to MoCA for patients with < 12 years of education
- Mini-Cog (3 minutes): 3-item recall + clock drawing. Score 0–2 = positive screen for cognitive impairment

Step 5: Functional assessment
- ADLs (Katz Index): bathing, dressing, toileting, transferring, continence, feeding
- IADLs (Lawton): telephone, shopping, food preparation, housekeeping, laundry, transport, medications, finances
- Decline in IADLs before ADLs is typical in early dementia

Step 6: Safety assessment
- Driving ability
- Medication management
- Financial management
- Home safety (fire, wandering, falls)
- Nutrition and hydration

sections.title: Nursing Interventions
sections.content:
Delirium Prevention — HELP Program (Hospital Elder Life Program):
- Orientation: clock, calendar, whiteboard with date/place/nurse name, daily orientation conversations
- Therapeutic activities: cognitively stimulating activities 3×/day
- Early mobilization: get out of bed within 24 hours of admission; chair sitting at meals
- Vision: ensure glasses are available and clean; adequate lighting
- Hearing: ensure hearing aids in and working; reduce background noise; face patient when speaking
- Hydration: daily fluid intake monitoring; early recognition and treatment of dehydration
- Sleep: non-pharmacological sleep protocol (warm milk, relaxation music, back massage, avoid nighttime interruptions)
- Pain management: assess using pain scale or behavioral indicators; untreated pain precipitates delirium
- Medication review: daily review of all medications; deprescribe anticholinergics, benzodiazepines, opioids if possible
- HELP program reduces delirium incidence by 33%

Delirium Management:
- Maintain safe environment: bed in low position, call bell within reach, non-skid footwear
- Reorientation without confrontation: calm, brief, factual statements
- Familiar objects from home: photos, a familiar blanket
- Minimize relocations: consistent room assignment, consistent nursing staff
- Family presence: encourage family to stay; they are therapeutic
- Physical restraints: AVOID — worsen delirium, increase injury risk, cause psychological trauma
- Pharmacological: only if patient is a safety risk; haloperidol 0.5 mg (lowest effective dose, shortest duration); BLACK BOX WARNING in dementia patients

Depression Management:
- Therapeutic communication: active listening, validation, non-judgmental presence
- Activity scheduling: structured daily routine including physical activity
- Social engagement: group activities, volunteer visitors, chaplain services
- Physical activity: aerobic exercise has antidepressant effect equivalent to medications
- Nutrition support: depression impairs appetite; small frequent meals, preferred foods
- Sleep hygiene: consistent sleep-wake schedule, limit napping
- Medication adherence support: pillboxes, calendar reminders, blister packs
- Monitor for suicidal ideation at every contact
- Antidepressants: SSRIs preferred (sertraline 25–50 mg, escitalopram 5–10 mg); response takes 4–6 weeks; counsel patient and family

Dementia Care:
- Person-centered care: use patient's preferred name, life history, interests
- Communication: simple language, one instruction at a time, allow processing time, non-verbal cues
- Environment: minimize clutter, consistent layout, adequate lighting, reduce noise
- Routine: predictable daily schedule reduces anxiety and behavioral symptoms
- Engagement: meaningful activity matched to preserved abilities
- Dignity preservation in all ADL assistance

sections.title: Patient and Family Teaching
sections.content:
Family Education for Delirium:
- Explain that delirium is NOT the patient 'going crazy' — it is a medical emergency with a cause
- The fluctuating, confused behavior is caused by the medical illness/medication/metabolic problem
- Family presence is THERAPEUTIC — calm, reassuring, familiar voice reduces distress
- Do not argue with or correct delusions/hallucinations — simply redirect
- Report ANY change from baseline immediately
- After delirium: warn that 40% of older adults do not return to full pre-delirium cognitive baseline
- Some memory and functional effects may persist for months

Family Education for Dementia:
- Dementia is not normal aging but is not the person's fault
- The disease progresses — planning ahead for care transitions is essential
- Communication strategies: speak slowly, use simple sentences, maintain eye contact, use touch
- 'Entering their reality' — do not correct delusions; validate feelings
- Caregiver burden is real; caregiver mental health matters as much as patient care
- Community resources: Alzheimer Society, adult day programs, respite care
- Legal planning: power of attorney for property and personal care while patient has capacity
- Safety: stove locks, door alarms, medication management, driving cessation

Patient Education for Depression:
- Depression is a medical illness, not weakness or character flaw
- Antidepressants take 4–6 weeks to show full benefit; do not stop early
- Side effects are usually mild and improve; never stop without consulting the provider
- Psychotherapy (CBT, IPT) is as effective as medication for mild-moderate depression
- Lifestyle: exercise, social connection, sleep hygiene, reduction of alcohol
- Suicidal thoughts are a symptom of illness; report them immediately
- Safety planning: identify warning signs, coping strategies, contacts to call in crisis

sections.title: Documentation Requirements
sections.content:
Delirium Documentation:
- Document CAM assessment results at least every 8–12 hours and with any change
- Record specific behaviors (not just 'confused') — 'Patient pulling IV, calling out, not recognizing nurse; CAM positive: features 1, 2, 4 present'
- Document baseline cognitive status (from family interview or prior records)
- Record all delirium precipitants investigated and treated
- Document physician notification and response
- Document restraint alternatives tried before any physical restraint order
- Document patient safety measures implemented

Dementia Documentation:
- Document patient's baseline cognitive level with specific functional descriptors
- Record MoCA/MMSE score with date
- Document decision-making capacity assessments and outcomes
- Record advance directives, substitute decision-maker name and contact
- Document behavioral symptoms with ABC (Antecedent-Behavior-Consequence) framework
- Legal note: document that driving cessation discussion occurred (with date, who was present)

Depression Documentation:
- GDS-15 score with date
- Specific statements made by patient (quote directly when significant, e.g., suicidal statements)
- Suicidal ideation: document intent, plan, means, timeline if disclosed; document safety plan
- Mandatory reporting: document any required notifications (capacity concerns, elder abuse linked to depression, inability to self-care)
- Medication: document patient understanding of antidepressant purpose, dosing, and side effects
- Record functional impact of depression on ADLs/IADLs

sections.title: Clinical Pearls
content: Hypoactive delirium kills quietly: the patient who is suddenly too quiet, too cooperative, too sleepy — investigate FIRST, sedate NEVER.
The patient who complains loudly about their memory likely has depression or anxiety, not dementia. The patient with dementia typically denies or minimizes memory problems (anosognosia).
CAM requires inattention (Feature 2) — you cannot diagnose delirium without demonstrating impaired attention. Use the digit span (forward) or the RASS before calling CAM positive.
Pseudodementia clinical test: treat the depression and see if cognition improves. If it does, the 'dementia' was depression. If it doesn't, now reassess for true underlying NCD.

The GDS-15 was specifically designed for older adults and avoids somatic items that would generate false positives in medically ill patients (unlike PHQ-9 which counts fatigue, sleep, and appetite changes).
Post-delirium neuropsychological testing is invalid for up to 4–6 weeks after delirium resolution. Never stage dementia or make capacity determinations during or immediately after delirium.

Denies memory problems + objectively impaired = dementia
visualAnchors: Delirium = Thunderstorm (sudden, dramatic, clears up when treated)
Dementia = Slow tide going out (gradual, inexorable, does not come back)
Depression = Cloudy day (pervasive, dims everything, clears with treatment)

sections.title: Safety Alerts
content: 🚨 HYPOACTIVE DELIRIUM IS THE MOST DANGEROUS: quiet, withdrawn, sleepy patients are at equal or GREATER mortality risk than agitated patients. Do not mistake withdrawal for calm.

🚨 ANTIPSYCHOTICS CARRY FDA BLACK BOX WARNING in patients with dementia: increased risk of cerebrovascular events and all-cause mortality. Use ONLY as last resort, lowest dose, shortest duration, with full informed consent.

🚨 RESTRAINTS WORSEN DELIRIUM: physical restraints increase agitation, cause pressure injuries, increase fall risk when removed, and prolong delirium. Exhaust all non-pharmacological alternatives first.
🚨 NEVER SCREEN FOR DEMENTIA DURING ACTIVE DELIRIUM: MoCA and MMSE scores are invalid during delirium. Cognitive assessment must be deferred until the delirium fully resolves.

🚨 SUICIDAL IDEATION IN OLDER MEN IS ESPECIALLY LETHAL: older white men ≥85 have the highest suicide completion rate. Any suicidal statement requires immediate safety assessment and psychiatric referral — do not defer.

sections.title: Exam Tips
content: NCLEX/CNPLE discriminator: When a question describes an older adult who is 'suddenly confused' in the hospital — the answer is almost always delirium (treat the cause, do CAM). The words SUDDEN and ACUTE are your delirium triggers.

On SATA questions about delirium interventions: select reorientation, ensuring glasses/hearing aids are in place, encouraging family presence, promoting sleep, and reviewing medications. Do NOT select physical restraints or routine antipsychotics.
GDS-15 cutoffs appear frequently: ≥5 = positive screen; ≥10 = significant depression. Know both numbers.
CAM algorithm question: Features 1 AND 2 must BOTH be present. If a question gives you fluctuating behavior but the patient is attentive — that is NOT delirium by CAM.

Pseudodementia vs dementia questions: the patient who says 'I don't know' repeatedly and has inconsistent test performance, with a recent life stressor, is depression until proven otherwise. Key differentiator: response to antidepressant treatment.

When a question asks about a patient with known dementia who has acutely changed — always first rule out delirium. Dementia does not change acutely. Even in dementia, acute change = investigate for delirium cause.

Hypoactive delirium vs depression differentiation on exams: hypoactive delirium is ACUTE (days); depression is SUBACUTE (weeks). Hypoactive delirium has fluctuation and a physical cause; depression has consistent mood symptoms and no acute physical precipitant.
